title: RTX Corporation (RTX) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of RTX Corporation (RTX) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale: Explicit multi-year free cash flow projections are withheld. RTX operates under massive, multi-decade government contracts whose complex revenue recognition and cost-overrun structures make standard 5-year FCF forecasting highly unreliable.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: The discount rate is null. While its incredibly low beta of 0.406 would mathematically suggest a very low cost of equity, RTX's $39.9 billion debt load obscures a standard WACC calculation from top-level data.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: 3.0% represents a standard, conservative terminal rate reflecting the reality that a massive industrial conglomerate cannot outpace global GDP growth into perpetuity.

thesisParagraphs: RTX Corporation operates as a fundamental pillar of Western defense infrastructure and global commercial aviation. Formed by the mega-merger of Raytheon and United Technologies, its scale is virtually unassailable. Generating nearly $88.6 billion in revenue and $10.5 billion in operating cash flow, it benefits from an oligopoly structure where multi-decade government contracts guarantee long-term solvency.

However, this extreme stability comes at the cost of margin compression and intense capital requirements. The defense industry is essentially a subsidized utility—governments require peak technological innovation but enforce strict cost controls on the back end, capping gross margins (currently around 20%). While its current $270B+ market cap reflects deep geopolitical anxiety and a booming order backlog, true intrinsic valuation requires forecasting multi-decade government spending priorities, which lies beyond the scope of a standard DCF model.
verdictFooter: Based on latest actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: Why didn't Gemini calculate specific IV for RTX?
faq.answer: Defense conglomerates are poor candidates for standard DCF analysis. Their cash flows are dictated by political appropriations, classified program budgets, and complex percentage-of-completion accounting rules that cannot be accurately modeled from basic APIs.

faq.question: Does RTX carry a lot of debt?
faq.answer: Yes. Following its merger and subsequent reorganizations, RTX carries nearly $40 billion in total debt against $7.4 billion in cash, a significant leverage profile typical of heavy industrial manufacturing.

faq.question: What is RTX's dividend yield?
faq.answer: RTX offers a reliable 1.36% dividend yield with a 53.8% payout ratio, signaling management's commitment to returning capital to shareholders despite its heavy capital expenditure requirements.
